description:
You are the on-call Site Reliability Engineer (SRE).  
Your job is to set up a **very small, self-contained uptime-monitoring service** that can be run entirely from your unprivileged shell account located at /home/user.  
Nothing needs to be installed system-wide; everything must live under /home/user.

Objectives (all must be satisfied):

1. Directory layout  
   • Create a new directory called  
     /home/user/monitoring  
   • All files mentioned below have to live inside that directory.

2. Target list (configuration file)  
   • Inside /home/user/monitoring create a plain-text file called targets.txt.  
   • Each line must contain exactly one target in this form (no extra spaces):  
     NAME,TYPE,ADDRESS  
     Where  
       – NAME    = an arbitrary identifier with no spaces (e.g. example_com)  
       – TYPE    = either ICMP or HTTPS (case-sensitive)  
       – ADDRESS = the literal address that will be probed (for HTTPS this must be a full URL that <curl> understands; for ICMP this must be an IPv4 or hostname).  
   • For the purpose of this task, populate the file with exactly the following three lines, in this order:
     example_com,HTTPS,https://example.com
     cloudflare_dns,ICMP,1.1.1.1
     google_dns,ICMP,8.8.8.8

3. Monitoring script  
   • Create /home/user/monitoring/uptime_monitor.sh (must be executable).  
   • Behaviour:  
     a) Read targets.txt line-by-line.  
     b) For every target, launch the appropriate probe in parallel (background) while the script keeps going:  
        – HTTPS  →   curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "$ADDRESS"  
        – ICMP   →   ping -c1 -W2 "$ADDRESS"  
     c) Collect each probe’s exit status (0 = success, anything else = failure).  
     d) For every finished probe, immediately write **one line** to /home/user/monitoring/uptime.log in the exact CSV format defined below.  
     e) Wait for all background jobs to finish before the script itself exits.

4. Log file  
   • Path: /home/user/monitoring/uptime.log  
   • The file is created on the first run if it does not yet exist.  
   • **CSV output format (exactly this, comma-separated, no spaces):**  
     TIMESTAMP_ISO8601,NAME,STATUS  
     Where  
       – TIMESTAMP_ISO8601 = in UTC, produced by the command date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"  
       – NAME              = the NAME field from targets.txt  
       – STATUS            = the literal string UP when the probe succeeded, DOWN otherwise  
   • Example (your actual timestamps will of course differ):  
     2024-04-15T12:34:56Z,example_com,UP

5. Convenience wrapper  
   • Provide /home/user/monitoring/run_once.sh (executable).  
   • It must simply call uptime_monitor.sh once and then emit to STDOUT the last three lines of uptime.log.  
     - This minor wrapper is required so that automated grading can invoke a single command and immediately get fresh log output without needing to know your script’s internal details.

6. Idempotence & re-entrancy  
   • Re-running run_once.sh multiple times must keep appending to uptime.log; never overwrite or truncate it.  
   • The monitoring script must cleanly handle an existing uptime.log and must still log one new line per target per invocation.

7. What the evaluator will do  
   The automated test harness will:
   a) cd /home/user/monitoring  
   b) ./run_once.sh  
   c) Immediately inspect uptime.log and verify:  
      – It exists and is non-empty.  
      – The last three lines match the CSV schema and contain the three required NAME values (order not important).  
      – Every STATUS field is either UP or DOWN (case-sensitive).  
   Therefore, make sure your implementation satisfies those checks.

Remember:  
• Do not use sudo or anything that requires elevated privileges.  
• Place no files outside /home/user/monitoring.  
• All scripts must have the executable bit set.  
• You may assume curl and ping exist inside the container.  

In summary: build the directory, configuration file, monitoring script, log file, and wrapper as specified so the evaluator’s checks pass.
truth:
Initial conditions before the agent starts:
• There is no /home/user/monitoring directory.

Files the agent must have created by the end:

1. /home/user/monitoring/targets.txt  
   Exact contents (including final newline):
example_com,HTTPS,https://example.com
cloudflare_dns,ICMP,1.1.1.1
google_dns,ICMP,8.8.8.8

2. /home/user/monitoring/uptime_monitor.sh  
   Must be executable (mode 755).  
   Not inspected for exact body, only for:
   – It exists and is executable.

3. /home/user/monitoring/run_once.sh  
   Must be executable (mode 755).  
   Not inspected for exact body, but evaluator will invoke it as described.

4. /home/user/monitoring/uptime.log  
   After evaluator’s single call to run_once.sh, the evaluator expects:
   – The last three lines of the file (tail -n 3) each match regex:
     ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z,(example_com|cloudflare_dns|google_dns),(UP|DOWN)$
   – All three unique NAME values must appear in those last three lines (order ignored).

Permissions:
• The /home/user/monitoring directory and all files inside it must be owned by the unprivileged user (uid 1000, gid 1000 inside container) and writable.

No other files, directories or processes are required or will be checked.
